concise:
Ayah 1–11 · linked video: 1. Al-’Adiyat (Ayah 1-11)

Surah Al-Adiyat: Commentary on Ayahs 1-11

Ayah 1

وَٱلْعَـٰدِيَـٰتِ ضَبْحًا

The surah opens with a dramatic oath, a scene so vivid that it immediately captures the imagination of its original audience. To understand الْعَـٰدِيَـٰتِ, we must explore the rich meanings contained within this word. The root ع د و carries multiple dimensions: to cross a limit, to act aggressively, to move with intense speed, and even to snatch or kidnap with overwhelming force.

The word عَادِيَة in classical Arabic refers to a cavalryman, though it can also denote the horse itself. The context here, with the mention of ضَبْحًا (panting), clarifies that these are the horses themselves. The imagery is of a small band of cavalry - the first wave of an attack force. The ال at the end signifies a limited number, suggesting these are brave souls risking their lives at the forefront of battle.

What makes this opening so powerful is how it appeals to the Arabs’ deep connection with their horses and their intimate knowledge of raids and warfare. Allah ﷻ is swearing by horses that are charging aggressively, obsessively pursuing their target, crossing every boundary in their path. These aren’t casual riders - these are warriors who have been riding through the night, their horses now exhausted but still pushing forward with unrelenting determination.

The word ضَبْحًا deserves special attention. It describes the heavy panting of horses breathing from their chests after intense exertion. But the word carries additional meanings: it’s used for the sound a bow makes when an arrow is released - that swift whoosh of something moving at incredible speed. It’s also used to describe something scorched by fire, capturing the English expression of being “burned” or “blazed” in a race. This single word conveys not just exhaustion, but blinding speed that leaves everything else behind.

The grammatical form used here (ism fa’il) suggests continuous action - these horses have been at this for a while. They started their charge the night before, and they’re still going, their breath growing heavier with each passing moment.

Ayah 2

فَٱلْمُورِيَـٰتِ قَدْحًا

The فَ at the beginning signals consequence: as a result of that intense galloping comes this next scene. The horses are now causing sparks to fly - الْمُورِيَـٰتِ قَدْحًا. When horses gallop at full speed over rocky terrain, their hooves striking against stone create sparks.

But the word مُورِيَـٰت carries deeper implications. In Arabic, when people say “a spark that never turned into a flame,” they’re describing something that showed potential but never materialized - like a friendship that never developed or a business venture that failed. Here, Allah ﷻ reverses that idiom. These aren’t sparks that fizzle out. The root ورى suggests something building up, swelling to the point of bursting. These sparks are so intense they threaten to become actual flames.

The word قَدْح refers to stones that easily produce fire when struck. The image is of horses moving so fast that they’re turning the earth beneath them into a field of matches, leaving burn marks on the rocks, almost setting the ground ablaze. If in the first ayah we heard about speed that could burn, now we see what’s actually being scorched - the earth itself is left with marks that won’t fade.

The camera of this scene has shifted. First, we heard the panting. Now we see the hooves striking, sparks flying, and as these horses whiz past, burn marks remain on the ground as testimony to their passage.

Ayah 3

فَٱلْمُغِيرَٰتِ صُبْحًا

Another consequence unfolds: then they raid at dawn - الْمُغِيرَٰتِ صُبْحًا. The word مُغِيرَات comes from غور, which means to descend, to go deep, to examine something thoroughly. It’s used for water that goes underground, for eyes that sink into the face, and crucially, for cavalry charging downhill from high ground into the lowlands.

This detail is strategically significant. Descending adds even more speed - gravity itself aids their charge. But there’s more: the word غَارَة is related to the rising sun. In fact, أَشْرَقَ means a house facing sunrise, and the morning itself is called الْغَدَاة in Arabic. These horses aren’t just descending; they’re coming from where the sun rises, with the morning light behind them.

The explicit mention of صُبْحًا (morning time) is deliberate. Raids typically happen at night when darkness provides cover. But these warriors are charging in broad daylight, in the morning, from the direction of the rising sun. They’re completely visible to the enemy. If they’re descending from high ground, they can be seen from far away. Strategically, this seems suicidal.

The sparks we discussed earlier are harder to see in daylight than in darkness, yet they’re so intense they’re visible even in the morning light. Everything about this raid defies conventional military wisdom. They’re few in number, they’re attacking in daylight, they’re coming from the most visible direction, and they’re not using any element of surprise. They’re charging straight at their enemy with complete visibility.

For the Arabs listening to this, their hearts would race. They knew these raids intimately - the thrill, the danger, the honor. The Quran is describing something they deeply understood and valued, using vocabulary that speaks directly to their experience.

Ayah 4

فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِۦ نَقْعًا

Now the pace of the narrative shifts. Up until now, the ayahs used noun forms (ism), suggesting continuous, prolonged action - the long journey, the persistent galloping, the extended panting. But suddenly we encounter verbs in the past tense - فَأَثَرْنَ. This grammatical shift is deliberate. Verbs capture immediate, decisive action. The clash is happening now, in split seconds.

The word أَثَرْنَ means to leave a mark, to make a dent, to create an impression that cannot be erased. It’s used for making an incision in a camel’s hoof, for creating footprints, for leaving ruins. The Arabs would say “أَثَّرَ فِيَّ” (he left a mark on me) when someone’s words scarred their dignity permanently. It’s the same root as أَثَر - the narrations from the Companions (RA) - suggesting words that have left an indelible impact.

What are these horses leaving marks on? The word نَقْعًا refers to water that remains stagnant in ditches or hollows - places difficult to traverse, areas people avoid. This detail tells us these horses came through rough, impassable terrain. They took routes others wouldn’t dare attempt.

But نقع also means something that rises up. Here it describes clouds of dust rising as the horses charge. We’re in desert terrain - rock and sand. As the cavalry descends at full speed, enormous dust clouds billow up. From a distance, you’d see this massive cloud emerging, hear a rumbling sound, unable to make out what’s inside.

Imam Al-Razi (may Allah have mercy on him) offers a brilliant insight: it’s morning, when there’s humidity and the ground is wet with dew. Normally, wet soil doesn’t create much dust. But these horses are moving so violently that despite the morning moisture, clouds of dust still rise. Another reading suggests the sand is behaving like water - imagine horses splashing through water, how it jumps and sprays. That’s what the desert sand looks like now, splashing up like ocean waves as they charge through.

The pronoun بِهِ (“by means of it”) is significant. One interpretation: despite it being morning, despite being visible, despite all odds against them, they’re still causing this chaos. Another: as a result of everything that’s come before - the long night ride, the descent, the speed - now finally the dust clouds rise.

Now there’s a level of invisibility created. Yes, it’s daytime and the enemy could see them coming, but suddenly there’s confusion. The dust obscures vision. In that confusion comes the final movement.

Ayah 5

فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِۦ جَمْعًا

They penetrated the middle - فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِۦ جَمْعًا. The word وَسَط means to come right into the center of something. Think of a spear piercing through flesh, or a bowling ball crashing through pins, or a truck smashing through a barrier. These few horses formed like a spearhead and drove straight through the enemy ranks.

Row after row of enemy soldiers, and they kept pushing through until they reached the dead center. They used the dust cloud to their advantage, creating confusion about their numbers and position. The enemy, surrounding them, bloodthirsty and ready, couldn’t track them until suddenly - they’re in the middle.

The word جَمْعًا suggests a large gathering. This isn’t a small force; it’s a massive enemy army, row upon row. Yet this tiny cavalry penetrated all the way through to the center. Some scholars interpret جمع as referring to the horses themselves staying together, riding as one unified force. But the stronger interpretation, given the Quran’s usage of this word elsewhere, is that it refers to the large gathering of enemy forces that this small band somehow penetrated.

The camera has taken us to the dead center. The enemy surrounds them on all sides. Their spears are raised. And then… the scene cuts.

What happened next? Did they survive? Did they fight their way out? Did the second wave arrive? We don’t know. Allah ﷻ doesn’t tell us. The scene ends on this cliffhanger, this moment of ultimate tension.

This is the end of Section One. The Arabs listening would be on the edge of their seats. If this were a movie trailer, this would be the moment the screen goes black and you see: “Coming Soon.” Your curiosity is at its peak, and then Allah ﷻ completely changes the subject.

Ayah 6

إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لِرَبِّهِۦ لَكَنُودٌ

Without any transition, we move to Section Two. The dramatic scene ends, and now comes direct address: indeed, the human being is truly ungrateful to his Master - إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لِرَبِّهِۦ لَكَنُودٌ.

The word إِنسَان comes from نسيان - forgetfulness. Human beings are fundamentally forgetful creatures. And the word كَنُود is devastating in its implications. Let me unpack its meanings.

Kanud describes land that is barren, that produces nothing of value. Applied to humans, it means someone who is of no use to their master. Think about it: what good is a horse that refuses to run, that won’t march toward the enemy? It’s worthless. It becomes just meat and bones. When you’re of no use to your master, what justifies your existence?

Kanud also means to cut off ties, to disconnect. In classical Arabic, they would describe a young man who cuts off his father - the one who raised him, provided for him, to whom he owes his very bloodline - and instead becomes close to his maternal uncles just to spite his father. This betrayal, this switching of loyalty, this is kanud.

Another layer: kanud means extremely ungrateful, forgetting the favors done for you. It describes someone who is miserly, greedy, unable to let go because they’re obsessed with getting and keeping. And ultimately, it means disobedient - someone who remains in disobedience because they can’t sacrifice their sins.

The statement is emphatic: إِنَّ for emphasis, the لام before رَبِّهِۦ for emphasis, another لام before كَنُودٌ. Allah ﷻ is hammering this point home: truly, without doubt, human beings are intensely ungrateful to their Master.

Now, what’s the connection to the scene we just witnessed? The horse has every instinct to run away from danger. It sees the spears, smells death, yet it charges forward. Why? Because you fed it, sheltered it, cared for it. You probably slaughtered its parents in front of it and ate them. Yet for those minimal acts of provision, this horse demonstrates such loyalty that it overrides its most basic survival instinct.

The horse will sacrifice itself for you. You bet your life on your horse’s loyalty - if it falters, if it hesitates, you fall and die. Your horse is an extension of yourself in that moment.

And Allah ﷻ is saying: Learn from your horse. Look at what minimal care produces in terms of loyalty. Now look at yourself. Has Allah ﷻ given you more than you’ve given your horse? Has He provided more reason to be loyal? Has He asked you to literally march into spears? What is it that makes you so ungrateful, so disloyal?

There’s another dimension here. These Arabs loved raiding. They made poetry about their crimes - the villages they pillaged, the people they robbed. It was their pride, their street credibility. The Quran gave them imagery better than any poetry they could compose, and they’re listening, enthralled, relating to it completely because this is their life.

But every one of those raids was an act of disobedience to Allah ﷻ. Murder, theft, pillaging - all crimes, all sins. إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لِرَبِّهِۦ لَكَنُودٌ - when humans are overwhelmed by greed, when they’re in the middle of committing sin, who do they cut off? They cut Allah ﷻ off. They disconnect.

I once spoke to someone about their habit of attending concerts. “Why do you go? It’s not appropriate.” They said, “It’s not that bad.” I pressed: “Alcohol, inappropriate music, people staring at each other wrongly - what’s happening there?” Finally, they admitted: “Allah is missing for a few hours.”

Exactly. You disconnect. For those hours, you check out, like He doesn’t exist. Someone can believe in Allah ﷻ, accept the Quran, accept the Prophet ﷺ, and still reach points where they completely disconnect, where nothing stops them. The Quraysh believed in Allah ﷻ - ask them who created the heavens and earth, they’d declare emphatically that Allah ﷻ did. Yet when it came to their sins, they cut Him off.

You see this in criminals worldwide - mafia members, cartel leaders who are staunch churchgoers. They have crucifixes, they’re “religious,” but when it’s time to commit their crimes, they disconnect completely. Then they return to their religion afterward.

The earth that is kanud produces nothing. When it comes to your Master, you become worthless, of no value, no use.

Ayah 7

وَإِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ

And truly, he is a witness to that - وَإِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ. Who is the best witness to how ungrateful, how greedy, how disobedient you can become? You yourself. Nobody knows better than you.

Notice Allah ﷻ doesn’t say شَاهِد (one who witnesses once). He says شَهِيد (constantly witnessing). Ask yourself at any moment, and if you’re honest, you can testify to your own failures. This is your constant state.

Unless, of course, you’re kanud - unless you’ve cut Allah ﷻ off and don’t want to think about it. But if you truly reflect, there’s not a moment where you can’t honestly confess your shortcomings to yourself.

This ayah creates a beautiful symmetry in this middle section. The section began with your disloyalty to Allah ﷻ, your lack of gratitude, your misplaced obsession. If your obsession isn’t correctly directed toward Allah ﷻ, then where is it?

Ayah 8

وَإِنَّهُۥ لِحُبِّ ٱلْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ

And indeed, he is intense in his love of wealth - وَإِنَّهُۥ لِحُبِّ ٱلْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ. The word شَدِيد means extremely tight, unable to be broken, like bonds on a captive that can’t be loosened. It’s used for severe punishments that cannot be undone, for the strength of maturity from which you don’t return to childhood. You are set in your ways, extremely intense.

Intense about what? Out of your love of الْخَيْر. The word literally means “good,” but in this context, it refers to wealth. The Quran uses it this way elsewhere: “It is prescribed for you, when death approaches one of you and he leaves behind خَيْر (wealth), that he make a bequest” (2:180). And: “Those to whom We have given wealth and children, We have hastened good for them” (23:55-56).

But خَيْر isn’t used for just any money. It’s used for abundant wealth, for lots of money. You could be on minimum wage with almost nothing in your pocket, but you’re constantly dreaming of millions, thinking about Rolls Royces, seven-star hotels, private jets. You don’t have to be wealthy to be materialistic or to have intense love of wealth. You could have nothing and constantly think about money. Conversely, you could have everything and it doesn’t affect your heart.

Allah ﷻ isn’t concerned with what you have in your hand. He’s concerned with what you have in your heart.

And it’s not just about money. You could be a janitor at a hotel working on the second floor, and someone on the third floor has a new vacuum cleaner, and you’re jealous. You could be in an office, and someone got a chair with lumbar support while you still have the old chair, and you’re resentful. You could be in a classroom, and your chair has a scratch, and during break you switch with someone else’s.

This isn’t just about wealth. It’s about الْخَيْر - wanting the very best of whatever you could possibly have. Never being satisfied with what you have. Always wanting more, wanting better. Not satisfied with your car, your clothes, your house, your possessions. As children, never satisfied with your toys, your games. Never satisfied with your physical appearance.

The لام in لِحُبِّ indicates causation: because of his love of wealth, he becomes truly intense. How does this happen? Look at the Arab in the opening scene. He’s willing to risk his own life, ride into almost certain death, because he’s dreaming of the treasure he might capture. That’s pretty intense testimony to how much he loves wealth, isn’t it?

People who could work regular jobs and be comfortable instead work 70, 80, 100 hours a week, killing themselves for that career, that position, that amount of money. They invest everything - time, health, relationships - into accumulating wealth. Many multi-millionaires end up as patients in hospitals, in comas from what they’ve done to themselves.

When these people do have time off, how do they cope? They get drunk, they get high, they go to mind-numbing clubs and lose themselves. Why? Because they’ve given 99% of their time to money, so they have no real human connections left, no genuine relationships. They can’t live with themselves, with their actual selves, so they need to forget who they are. They burn those brain cells away to avoid remembering what happened the night before.

There’s no lower life than that “high life.”

The Quraysh lived for these raids, these crimes they committed with great pride. The Quran captures their imagination, almost endorsing their excitement, and then delivers this devastating diagnosis.

Ayah 9

أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ

Does he not know what will happen when what is in the graves is overturned - أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ? Now we enter Section Three, the conclusion, and suddenly we’re back to the imagery of raiding.

The word بُعْثِرَ is extraordinary. Think about when you’re looking for something in a cluttered drawer - you scatter things around, flip things over, pull items out, make piles, ransack the entire space trying to find one specific item. Maybe you’re looking for a ring in a huge jewelry box, turning everything upside down. This act of ransacking, of turning everything inside out in search of something valuable - this is بُعْثِرَ.

What’s inside graves that would require ransacking? Graves that are thousands of years old contain dirt, decayed bones. Yet Allah ﷻ says they will be ransacked, implying there’s something of immense value hidden there that needs to be found.

The verb is in the passive form: “when it is ransacked.” Who will do the ransacking? Allah ﷻ deliberately leaves this mysterious. But here’s what’s profound: This surah started with a raid. You, as the Arab listener, were proud to be the raider. Now you’re being told your grave will be ransacked. Wait - I’m the raider. Who’s going to raid me?

You thought this was about raids you conduct? No. There’s another army of raiders coming, and they will descend when the sun rises - but from the west. It will be a morning unlike any other. When they raid, clouds of dust will rise again. They’ll go straight through all of you.

Look at the parallels. When raiders finish destroying the enemy, what do they look for? The treasure. Where is treasure buried? Underground. So they start digging everywhere, searching. When they hit something, they open it up to find what’s valuable inside. Maybe there are a hundred worthless items, but one precious diamond.

That’s exactly what’s being described here. The real raid is coming, and the question is: when your chest is opened, what will be found inside?

Notice: when graves are toppled, dust rises again, doesn’t it? Just like in the opening scene. Some of the greatest ancient tombs have massive structures, columns, statues built on top of them. Many ancient kings buried themselves with their treasure. The Arabs didn’t have as much treasure to bury, and those left behind weren’t about to waste it, so they’d tie the dead chief’s camel to the grave and let it starve as a symbolic gesture.

Ayah 10

وَحُصِّلَ مَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ

And when what is in the chests is extracted - وَحُصِّلَ مَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ. This word حُصِّلَ appears nowhere else in the Quran. It comes from حصل, which means something buried that nobody knew existed, hidden away for a very long time.

Classical Arabs used حصل for pebbles that camels accidentally eat with their food. These pebbles stay in the camel’s stomach for its entire life, and when the animal is finally slaughtered and its stomach is opened, these pebbles are discovered - nobody even knew they were there. That’s حصل.

Think about when you’re moving furniture and you pull out the couch cushions and find something you lost years ago - “Oh my God, I forgot this was here!” That’s حصل.

Allah ﷻ is saying something remarkable: Someone died thousands of years ago. What was in their heart, nobody knows. People don’t even remember these individuals existed, let alone know what was in their hearts. Yet that treasure - the contents of that heart - will be fully retrieved.

The word also has another usage: حُصَالَة refers to the leftover stalks after crops are harvested, the part people trample over that has no value. Nations came and went like crops being cut. What’s left of them is underground. Nobody knows them, nobody cares about them, they’re not mentioned anymore. The vast majority have no imprint left on the world.

Yet Allah ﷻ says whatever was in their chests has value to Him, and on Judgment Day, it will be brought up.

Notice the connection: earlier, Allah ﷻ said your love of wealth is really intense - لِحُبِّ ٱلْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ. The chest is where love resides. Now, when that chest is pulled out, when that heart is extracted, will Allah ﷻ find حُبّ (love) of wealth inside it? That’s the question being posed.

Who will pull people from their graves? There’s no explicit answer in the Quran. Some ayahs hint at angels: “Bring yourselves out!” the angels will command (6:93). Allah ﷻ won’t speak directly to criminals on that Day - so who’s speaking? The angels. Perhaps they’re the ones conducting this raid.

But here’s the crucial difference between the two raids: In the first raid, when the pillagers conquered, did they know exactly where the treasure was? No, they had to search. Even when they found a chest, they might be disappointed with its contents.

But when Allah ﷻ retrieves what’s in the chests - وَحُصِّلَ مَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ - He will know exactly where to look and exactly what He’ll find.

Ayah 11

إِنَّ رَبَّهُم بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّخَبِيرٌۢ

Indeed, their Master will be, on that Day, fully aware - إِنَّ رَبَّهُم بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّخَبِيرٌۢ. Not just عَالِم (knowing), but خَبِير. Both words mean “knowing,” but there’s a profound difference.

The origin of خَبِير is remarkable. It comes from the source of a waterfall. You can know where the waterfall is, you can film where the waterfall is, but if you have خبر of the waterfall, you know where the water reservoir inside the mountain is, where it actually originates. You haven’t just seen the waterfall - you’ve dug into the mountain, explored it, experienced its source.

خَبِير also means to test something thoroughly, to verify its authenticity. I might say “I know that student” - maybe I know his name, maybe we met once, we talked. But if I taught him, tested him, corrected him, spent years with him, and then say “I know that student,” there’s a difference. When I’ve experienced that student completely, I have خبر of him.

On that Day, Allah ﷻ will have خبر - complete, experiential knowledge - of these people. They won’t be able to spin stories: “No, it was like this!” Allah ﷻ will say, “It wasn’t like this.” He won’t just have علم (knowledge) of you. He’ll have خُبْرَة (complete awareness) of you on that Day.

Remember the word العاديات from the very beginning? One of its derivatives is عَادٍ - the messenger of a king who goes out to arrest criminals so they can be taken back for trial. That’s part of the meaning embedded in the opening word.

Do you see it now? This surah has two dimensions. Yes, there’s a worldly image of a raid. But there’s also the image of the next life. Those عاديات will be the angels, and they will grab human beings and take them back - like an عادٍ - before Allah ﷻ for judgment.

And what happens when they stand before Allah ﷻ? The last ayah tells us: their Master will be fully aware - خَبِير - of them then, because they’ve been brought back by the عاديات of Allah ﷻ.

This is how the beginning and end of the surah are perfectly tied together.

One final note about morning: I mentioned earlier that morning is significant. Throughout the Quran, morning is the time of divine retribution. When Allah ﷻ destroyed the nation of ’Ad (AS): “By morning, nothing could be seen but their dwellings” (46:25). The people of Thamud: “The blast seized them at sunrise” (15:73). The people of Lut (AS): “Their appointed time was the morning” (54:38). When the Prophet ﷺ prepared his armies for the Battle of Badr, he positioned them in the morning.

Morning is the time of judgment, the time when truth becomes manifest. When the Raiders in this surah raid in the morning, it foreshadows another morning - the morning when the sun rises from the west, when the final raid commences, when what was hidden in hearts is revealed, when the real judgment begins.

The surah presents three sections: the raid at the beginning, the final raid at the end, and in the middle - comments about human beings who aren’t ready for that final raid. They’re too busy obsessed with worldly raids, completely blind to the raid that’s coming.

Three criticisms are leveled in the middle section: First, human ingratitude and disregard - كَنُود. Second, you witness your own wrongdoing and still don’t care - شَهِيد. Third, your love of what you think is best completely outweighs any reasonable judgment - شَدِيد.

This is Allah’s diagnosis, essentially saying: this is why you’re like the people being criticized in the raid, and not like those who can escape the raid that’s coming.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from being among the ungrateful. May He make us from those whose hearts, when they are extracted on that Day, are found to contain love for Him above all else. May He make us from those who are ready for that final morning, prepared with gratitude, loyalty and sincere devotion.
deep:
Ayah 1–11 · linked video: 1. Al-’Adiyat (Ayah 1-11)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

الْعَادِيَاتِ (al-’adiyat) [ع-د-و]

The root ع-د-و carries multiple profound meanings that build the imagery of this opening ayah. The primary meaning is to pass over something, to cross a limit or border (’adawa). The word encompasses aggression and obsession - when someone stalks another relentlessly, the Arabs would say “turn away from me” using this root. It signifies crossing boundaries inappropriately in speech, tone or action.

The word also implies theft, robbery and pillaging in classical Arabic. It was used for mounted raiders who would kidnap people while riding past, snatching them suddenly. The root inherently conveys intense speed - faster than the legendary horse Silaq in Arab poetry. It describes a horse that is difficult to manage due to its aggressive nature and overwhelming speed.

Crucially, al-’adiyat (the feminine plural form) refers to cavalrymen or the horses themselves in battle, distinguishing mounted soldiers from foot soldiers (ghuzat). The context of “panting” (dabhan) in the next phrase clarifies this refers specifically to the horses, not the riders. The grammatical form (ism fa’il) suggests continuous action - these horses have been galloping at full speed through the night, which explains their exhausted panting state.

The word ’adi was also used for bounty hunters - those who capture criminals to bring them before a king for judgment. This dual dimension is critical: the image works both for worldly raids and for the angels who will “raid” graves on Judgment Day to bring souls before Allah for reckoning.

ضَبْحًا (dabhan) [ض-ب-ح]

The root ض-ب-ح captures the sound and intensity of the horses’ breathing. It specifically refers to heavy breathing from the chest, distinct from ordinary breathing (nafas). The word was used for the sound a bow makes when released - that sharp, swift sound of something moving incredibly fast. This connects the panting to the speed: you only hear the sound as they blaze past you.

Remarkably, dabaha was also used when something is scorched by fire just enough to change its color. This creates the English equivalent of “you got burned” or “scorched” in a race - left far behind in the dust. The word choice emphasizes they are running at absolutely maximum speed, breathing as hard as physically possible. The grammatical construction (masdar as hal) intensifies this - describing the extreme degree of their panting, meaning they couldn’t possibly be breathing any harder than this.

Ayah 2

الْمُورِيَاتِ (al-muriyat) [و-ر-ي]

The root و-ر-ي fundamentally means to cause sparks or to ignite. Al-wuriya refers specifically to sparks that could potentially turn into flame. The Arabs had an idiom: “a spark that never turned into flame” - meaning a friendship with no chemistry or a business venture that never took off. Allah reverses this idiom here: these are sparks (muriyat) from the horseshoes striking rock that are so intense they almost do turn into flame (qadhan).

The word wariya also implies swelling or building up to something severe - like water boiling until it explodes, or a swelling inside the body that could burst. When Arabs saw an enemy cough, they would curse: “May your lungs swell and burst” (waribat ri’atuka). The word choice suggests these sparks are building toward something catastrophic - almost setting the ground on fire.

قَدْحًا (qadhan) [ق-د-ح]

The root ق-د-ح refers to stones that easily produce sparks when struck - essentially describing flint or similar fire-starting stones. The horses are turning the very earth into matches as they gallop. The word emphasizes the intensity of their speed and the force with which their hooves strike the ground. This isn’t casual movement - it’s violent, purposeful charging that leaves physical marks.

Ayah 3

الْمُغِيرَاتِ (al-mughirat) [غ-و-ر]

The root غ-و-ر has layered meanings essential to understanding this scene. Primarily, it means to examine something deeply or to descend steeply. The word ghara describes water that has gone deep underground into low places, staying stagnant in ditches or hollows - places not easily traversed. This already suggests the horses are coming from difficult, unexpected terrain.

Ghara also means to descend from high ground to low - crucial because descending while galloping adds gravitational acceleration to already maximum speed. The horses aren’t just fast; they’re getting even faster as they descend the hill toward the enemy. The word was used for someone whose eyes sink deep into their face, emphasizing the “going low” aspect.

Interestingly, al-ghara is another Arabic word for the sun, and the phrase “this house was built facing sunrise” uses gharata. The word choice reveals these horses are coming from where the sun rises - from the east, from the direction of morning light.

صُبْحًا (subhan) [ص-б-ح]

The root ص-б-ح means morning time, but its inclusion here is deliberate and significant. Raids are tactically most effective under cover of night. By specifying subhan (morning), Allah emphasizes these raiders are charging in full daylight when they can be seen from far away. This is strategically disadvantageous - they’re visible, they’re few in number (as established by the grammar), and they’re descending from the sun-lit side where they’re most exposed.

The morning (subh) throughout the Quran is associated with divine judgment and retribution. Nations like ’Ad were destroyed “by morning” (7:78). The “loud cry took hold of them as the sun rose” (54:38). The Battle of Badr, where Allah sent angels, occurred in the morning. The choice of morning isn’t random - it foreshadows the “morning” of Resurrection when judgment comes.

Ayah 4

أَثَرْنَ (atharna) [أ-ث-ر]

The root أ-ث-ر means to leave a mark, trace or impression that remains. The word was used for making an incision in a camel’s hoof to fit a horseshoe - a permanent mark. Athar means footprints because they dent the earth. It also means ruins - the lasting marks of civilizations.

The word evolved to mean “to relate a narration from someone” (athar in hadith terminology) because their words left an indelible mark on you - like a scar on your soul. When someone insults you deeply, Arabs would say “athara fi karami” - he scarred my dignity. The marks these words leave are permanent, like the physical mark of a hook on a camel.

By using atharna here, Allah describes the horses leaving permanent marks on the ground as they charge - burn marks, dust, disturbance that won’t easily fade. This connects to the previous ayah’s imagery of scorching and sparks.

نَقْعًا (naq’an) [ن-ق-ع]

The root ن-ق-ع primarily refers to water that stays stagnant in a ditch, hollow or cavity for a long time - places not easily traversed. This usage already suggests the difficult terrain these horses are crossing. But naq’a also means something that rises up.

Scholars debated whether this refers to clouds of dust rising or water splashing. Imam al-Razi brilliantly synthesized both: in morning time, there’s humidity and the soil is wet, with puddles of water. Despite this, the horses are running so fast that clouds of dust rise anyway. Alternatively, the sand itself is being compared to water - imagine horses splashing through water and how it jumps up; that’s how the sand now behaves as they charge through the desert.

The imagery suggests they’re “diving into” the enemy ranks like diving into an ocean. The dust clouds serve a tactical purpose - creating confusion and invisibility despite it being daylight.

Ayah 5

وَسَطْنَ (wasatna) [و-س-ط]

The root و-س-ط means to penetrate into the middle or center of something. The word captures the image of a spear piercing through flesh, or a bowling ball crashing through pins, or a battering ram breaking through gates. These few horses have formed like a spearhead and penetrated row after row of enemy ranks until they’ve reached the very center.

The verb form (past tense) contrasts sharply with the ism fa’il forms used earlier (al-’adiyat, al-muriyat, al-mughirat). The nouns suggested ongoing preparation - they’d been readying, galloping, getting ready for a long time. The sudden switch to the past tense verb captures immediate, split-second action: the actual clash happened instantly. They executed their attack in a moment.

The tactical use of the dust cloud (naq’an) enabled this penetration - the enemy couldn’t see clearly where the attack was coming from until the horses were already in their midst.

جَمْعًا (jam’an) [ج-م-ع]

The root ج-م-ع means to gather or assemble. Some scholars interpreted jam’an to mean the horses stayed together as one unit during their charge - they didn’t disperse but rode as a unified force. However, the more prevalent interpretation is that jam’an refers to the large gathering of enemy forces - row after row after row of soldiers.

The word choice emphasizes the asymmetry: these few horses (indicated by the grammatical structures earlier) have penetrated into the midst of a massive assembled army. The imagery stops at the climactic moment - they’re surrounded by enemies on all sides, having made it to the center. What happens next? Allah doesn’t tell us. The scene cuts.

Ayah 6

إِنَّ (inna) [particle]

The emphatic particle inna (“indeed/truly”) marks the dramatic shift from the battle scene to the moral lesson. This isn’t a typical continuation - it’s an abrupt subject change that forces the listener to connect the vivid imagery they just experienced with a profound truth about human nature.

الْإِنسَانَ (al-insan) [أ-ن-س]

The root أ-ن-س relates to humans and humanity. The word insan comes from nisyan (forgetfulness) according to some etymologists - humans are the creatures who forget. Others connect it to uns (familiarity/companionship) - humans are social beings. Here, al-insan with the definite article means “the human being” generally - this is a universal statement about human nature, not about specific individuals.

لِرَبِّهِ (li-rabbihi) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means master, lord, sustainer, nurturer. Rabb is the One who raises, provides for, manages and maintains. The construction li-rabbihi (“to his Rabb”) is crucial - not just “to Allah” but specifically “to his Master.” This echoes the horse-and-master relationship in the opening imagery. The horse shows absolute loyalty to the master who feeds and shelters it, even sacrificing its life. But humans, despite receiving infinitely more from their Master, show the opposite quality.

لَكَنُودٌ (la-kanud) [ك-ن-د]

The root ك-ن-د has multiple devastating meanings that build upon each other. Kanud primarily means ungrateful, disloyal, and disobedient. But the word goes deeper: an ard kanud is land that produces nothing - it’s barren, useless. When applied to humans, it means someone who is “of no use” to their Master.

Kanud was used for a son who cuts ties with his father and, to spite him, stays close with his maternal uncles - switching loyalties, burning the relationship. This disloyalty to the one who gave you your bloodline itself is captured in kanud.

The word also meant someone extremely stingy and miserly (bakhil). The connection: when you forget Allah’s favors (ungrateful), you become miserly - grabbing whatever you can right now because you’re not trusting in Allah’s provision. You can’t hold back from sin because you’re too miserly to sacrifice temporary pleasure. This miseriness leads to disobedience (’asi).

Grammatically, la-kanud has the lam for emphasis (“truly is”) and kanud is an intensive form (fa’ul pattern) - not just disloyal but EXTREMELY disloyal, disconnected and ungrateful.

Ayah 7

وَإِنَّهُ (wa innahu) [particle]

The wa (“and”) connects this ayah to the previous one, while innahu (“and indeed he”) maintains the emphasis. This construction means: “And the truth is, he himself…” The pronoun “he” refers back to al-insan (the human being).

عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ (ala dhalika) [demonstrative]

The phrase “ala dhalika” means “upon that” or “concerning that” - referring back to the previous ayah’s indictment. The human being is ungrateful, disloyal and disobedient to his Master - and concerning that reality…

لَشَهِيدٌ (la-shahid) [ش-ه-د]

The root ش-ه-د means to witness, testify, or give testimony. Shahid is one who bears witness. The construction here is profound: the human being himself is a witness (shahid) to his own ingratitude and disloyalty. You cannot deny it about yourself if you’re honest.

The form shahid (rather than shahid - one-time witness) suggests a constant state - “always a witness.” At any moment, if you genuinely reflect on yourself, you must confess this truth. You are your own evidence against yourself. The only way to avoid this self-testimony is to be kanud - to cut Allah off from your consciousness, to not think about it.

Ayah 8

وَإِنَّهُ (wa innahu) [particle]

Once again, the emphatic construction: “And indeed he…” This is the third statement about human nature in this middle section (ayahs 6-8): (1) humans are ungrateful to their Master, (2) they witness this truth about themselves, and (3) they are extremely intense in their love of material good.

لِحُبِّ (li-hubb) [ح-ب-ب]

The root ح-ب-ب means love, affection, attachment. Hubb is deep love. The construction li-hubbi (“in his love of” / “because of his love of”) is causal - it explains WHY humans become kanud. The love of khair (discussed below) is what drives the ingratitude and disobedience.

الْخَيْرِ (al-khair) [خ-ي-ر]

The root خ-ي-ر means good, better, best. Khair is goodness, but in the superlative sense - the best possible thing. Most classical scholars interpreted al-khair here to mean wealth (mal), and this is supported by Quranic usage elsewhere. In 2:180, regarding inheritance: “if he leaves behind khair” clearly means wealth. In 100:8, Allah expedited “khair for them” - meaning wealth and children.

Crucially, khair isn’t used for small amounts - it implies abundant wealth, lots of money. But the word choice is broader than just “mal” (money): khair suggests “the best” of anything. A person can have no money but constantly dream of millions - thinking of Rolls Royces, private jets, seven-star hotels. You don’t have to be wealthy to be materialistic. Conversely, you can have everything and not be attached to it.

The word captures a mentality: always wanting “the better” version. Never satisfied with your car, house, clothes, job, appearance - always wanting the best, the upgrade, the next level. This applies even to small things: jealous of someone’s vacuum cleaner, their chair at work, their position, their validation on social media.

لَشَدِيدٌ (la-shadid) [ش-د-د]

The root ش-د-د means strong, intense, severe, tight. Shadid is the intensive form - extremely intense, severe beyond normal. The word was used for bonds on a captive that cannot be broken - completely secure, impossible to escape from.

The construction la-shadidun means “truly/indeed is extreme.” Humans are INTENSE - extreme, obsessed, completely consumed - in their love of khair. This intensity is what makes someone risk their life in a raid for potential wealth, or work themselves to death for a promotion, or destroy their health and relationships in pursuit of material success.

Shidda was used for severe punishments that cannot be undone, and for maturity (the prime of life) from which you cannot return to childhood - a permanent, severe state. The word suggests humans are set in their ways regarding this love - it’s not casual or passing, it’s a defining, imprisoning characteristic.

Ayah 9

أَفَلَا (a-fa-la) [particles]

The combination of interrogative hamza (a-), conjunction fa- (“then”), and negation la- creates a rhetorical question with force: “Does he not then…?” or “Is he not aware then…?” The fa- (“then”) connects what follows to everything that came before - given all that’s been said about human nature, this is the logical question.

يَعْلَمُ (ya’lamu) [ع-ل-م]

The root ع-ل-م means to know. Ya’lamu is the present/future tense: “does he know” or “will he know.” The verb choice is deliberate - this isn’t about past knowledge but about awareness of what’s coming.

إِذَا (idha) [particle]

Idha is a particle indicating future time - “when” (in the future with certainty). This shifts the entire surah from past imagery (the raid) and present reality (human ingratitude) to future certainty (the Resurrection).

بُعْثِرَ (bu’thira) [ب-ع-ث-ر]

The root ب-ع-ث-ر is extraordinary - used only here in the entire Quran. The word means to scatter things around when searching for something, to ransack, to turn things over and upside down looking for something valuable. Imagine searching frantically through a messy drawer for your passport - pulling things out, flipping things over, making piles, scattering everything about.

Ba’thara specifically suggests searching for something precious that’s hidden among worthless things - like looking for one ring in a huge jewelry box, ransacking through everything. The passive form bu’thira (“is ransacked”) deliberately doesn’t mention WHO does the ransacking - creating mystery. Who will raid the graves?

The parallel to the opening imagery is stunning: the surah began with raiders (al-’adiyat) who ransack an enemy town searching for buried treasure. Now graves will be ransacked searching for what’s inside them. The same word family (’adiya / ’adi as bounty hunter) connects: these raiders bring criminals to the king for judgment. The angels will be the ’adiyat who raid graves and bring souls before Allah for judgment.

The image of graves being violently overturned also evokes clouds of dust rising - just like in ayah 4 (atharna naq’an). The parallel scenes mirror each other.

مَا (ma) [particle]

Ma is a relative pronoun meaning “what” or “that which” - introducing the object of bu’thira. What is it that will be ransacked from the graves?

فِي الْقُبُورِ (fi al-qubur) [ق-ب-ر]

The root ق-ب-ر means grave, tomb, burial place. Al-qubur (plural: “the graves”) with fi (“in”) means “what is in the graves.” This raises the question: what’s in graves that’s so valuable it must be ransacked? Thousand-year-old graves contain only dirt and decayed bones. There must be something precious hidden among all that decay - something worth digging for.

Ayah 10

وَحُصِّلَ (wa hussila) [ح-ص-ل]

The root ح-ص-ل means to obtain, acquire, retrieve, or extract something of value. Hassala (Form II) means to extract or retrieve thoroughly - to get at what’s really there. The word hussila (passive, Form II, intensive) means “is thoroughly extracted/retrieved.”

Hasil in Arabic referred to pebbles that camels accidentally eat which stay in their stomach their entire lives - nobody knows they’re there. When the animal dies and is slaughtered, they cut open the stomach and discover these pebbles that were hidden away the whole time. This is hasil - something buried away that nobody knew about, hidden for a very long time.

The word was also used for the worthless part of crops left after harvest that people trample over (hasida). The parallel: these dead people are like trampled, harvested crops - nations that came and went, cut down by time. Most are not remembered; nobody knows about them anymore. Some names appear entertainingly in history books, but they’ve left no real imprint. They’re forgotten, trampled, worthless remains.

Yet Allah says: whatever was inside their hearts will be fully retrieved (hussila). Whatever was hidden away, forgotten, unknown to anyone - Allah will extract it, no matter how long it’s been buried. People who died thousands of years ago, whose very names are lost - the contents of their hearts will be brought out.

مَا (ma) [particle]

The relative pronoun “what” or “that which” - introducing the object that will be retrieved.

فِي الصُّدُورِ (fi al-sudur) [ص-د-ر]

The root ص-د-ر means chest, breast, or the front of something. Sadr (plural: sudur) is the chest - anatomically where the heart resides. The phrase “fi al-sudur” means “what is in the chests” - referring to the hearts and what they contain.

This connects back to ayah 8: “wa innahu li-hubbi al-khairi la-shadid” - he is intense in his love (hubb) of wealth. Love resides in the heart (qalb), which is in the chest (sadr). The imagery builds: your love of khair was in your chest, and that chest will be torn open and what’s inside will be pulled out. Will Allah find khair (goodness) or hubb al-khair (obsessive love of material things) inside that heart?

The word choice is profound: sadr can mean the treasure chest that raiders search for. These graves are being ransacked like chests - and the actual treasure chest is the sadr (chest cavity) containing the qalb (heart). The raiders in ayah 1-5 were searching for buried treasure underground. The angels will search graves (underground) for the treasure of the heart (inside the chest).

Ayah 11

إِنَّ (inna) [particle]

The emphatic particle inna (“indeed/truly”) introduces the final, authoritative statement. After describing the ransacking of graves and retrieval of hearts’ contents, Allah makes His declaration of His own knowledge.

رَبَّهُم (rabbahum) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means lord, master, sustainer. Rabbahum (“their Rabb”) with the attached pronoun “their” is significant - these are the same people described as “kanud li-rabbihi” (ungrateful to his Rabb) in ayah 6. The word Rabb appears in both places, creating a frame: you were disloyal to your Rabb; now your Rabb has full knowledge of you.

بِهِم (bihim) [particle + pronoun]

The preposition bi- with the pronoun -him (“them”) means “of them” or “concerning them.” This construction with khabir (below) creates the phrase “fully informed about them.”

يَوْمَئِذٍ (yawma’idhin) [ي-و-م]

The root ي-و-م means day. Yawma’idhin is a compound: yawma (“on the day”) + idhin (“that” - referring to the day just described when graves are ransacked and hearts retrieved). “On that Day” connects directly to the events of ayahs 9-10. This is the Day of Resurrection and Judgment.

لَخَبِيرٌ (la-khabir) [خ-ب-ر]

The root خ-ب-ر means to know, to be informed, to have news of. But khabir is far more than ’alim (knower). The word khabir suggests experiential knowledge - someone who has tested, verified, experienced the source of something.

The linguistic origins are beautiful: khabir comes from the origin of a waterfall. Anyone can see where the waterfall is (that’s ’ilm), but khabir means you know where the water reservoir inside the mountain is - you know the source, the origin. You’ve dug into the mountain, explored it, experienced where it actually comes from.

Khabir was used for testing the veracity of something. For example, “I know that student” could mean you met once. But if you’ve taught him, tested him, corrected him, spent years with him - then you have khibrah (experiential knowledge) of him. You’ve experienced the student, not just encountered him.

In Quranic usage, khabir is almost exclusively used for Allah’s knowledge - it’s His special attribute. Allah is al-Khabir - the One with complete, experiential, verified, source-level knowledge. On that Day, when the chests are opened and hearts extracted, Allah will have hussala (retrieved/extracted) everything from those hearts. He will have khibrah - experiential, tested, verified, complete knowledge.

The la- prefix adds emphasis: “truly is Khabir.” The form khabir (fa’il pattern) is intensive - not just knowledgeable but All-Aware, Fully-Informed, Completely-Experienced in knowledge of them.

The final image: In the first raid (ayahs 1-5), when the raiders ransack a town looking for treasure, they don’t know exactly where to look or what they’ll find. They have to search blindly - opening this chest or that chest, digging here or there, uncertain if they’ll find anything valuable. But when Allah’s “raiders” (angels) ransack the graves, Allah knows exactly where to look and exactly what will be found. There’s no uncertainty, no blind searching - only complete, perfect khibrah (experiential knowledge) of every heart that’s ever existed.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayahs 1-5: The Oath Structure

The opening five ayahs form a unified oath (qasam) structure, which is a distinctive rhetorical feature of many Makkan surahs. The oath begins with the particle wa- (و) repeated with each successive ayah, building momentum and intensity. Classical rhetoricians note that oaths by created things point to their Creator’s majesty and power.

The grammatical pattern shifts dramatically between ayahs 1-3 and ayahs 4-5. The first three ayahs use ism fa’il forms (al-’adiyat, al-muriyat, al-mughirat) - active participles that suggest continuous, ongoing action extending over time. These horses have been galloping throughout the night, panting for hours, causing sparks for an extended period. The grammatical choice captures duration and preparation - they’ve been at this for a while.

Ayahs 4-5 abruptly switch to past-tense verbs (atharna, wasatna). This rhetorical shift is masterful: when the actual clash begins, when dust rises and they penetrate enemy ranks, everything happens in a split second. Past tense verbs in Arabic often capture immediate, completed action. The contrast between prolonged preparation (isms) and instant execution (verbs) creates cinematic pacing - slow buildup, sudden action.

The definite article al- with each participle (al-’adiyat, al-muriyat, al-mughirat) is significant. Classical grammarians debated whether this indicates ’ahd dhihni (known to the mind) or jins (generic category). The context suggests both work: these are horses familiar to the Arab audience’s imagination, yet also representative of any such cavalry raid. The use of the feminine plural is notable - indicating these are specifically horses, not the male riders.

The accusative case endings (dabhan, qadhan, subhan, naq’an, jam’an) function as hal (circumstantial descriptions) and maf’ul mutlaq (cognate accusatives), intensifying the verbal action. “Dabhan” doesn’t just mean they’re panting - it means they’re panting to the maximum possible degree.

Ayah 6: The Abrupt Shift

The transition from ayah 5 to ayah 6 is one of the most dramatic rhetorical moves in the Quran. After the intense, breathless description of the raid, the scene abruptly cuts mid-action. The listener is left suspended - did the raiders survive? Did they conquer? This unresolved tension mirrors modern film trailers that show the most exciting scenes without revealing the outcome.

The particle inna (إِنَّ) signals an emphatic, independent statement - not grammatically connected to what preceded it. This creates what Arabic rhetoricians call iltifat (grammatical shift) at the level of theme and subject matter. From third-person description of horses, we shift to a universal second-person statement about human nature.

The lam in “la-kanud” (لَكَنُودٌ) is lam al-ibtida’ - the emphatic lam that stresses the predicate. Combined with inna, this creates double emphasis: “Indeed, truly, the human being is ungrateful to his Master.” The khabar (predicate) comes with lam to emphasize the quality of kanud as an inherent, definitive human characteristic.

The attachment of the pronoun “his” (li-rabbihi) rather than just saying “to Allah” creates intimacy and ownership - this is YOUR master, the One who owns YOU specifically. This personal pronoun intensifies the betrayal of ingratitude.

Ayah 7: Witness Against Self

The repetition of “wa innahu” (وَإِنَّهُ) creates parallelism with ayah 6, building a three-part indictment across ayahs 6-8. Each begins with the same emphatic structure, creating rhythmic emphasis.

The construction “’ala dhalika” (عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ) uses the demonstrative pronoun to point back anaphorically to the previous statement. This is a common Quranic device for cohesion - “upon that” refers to everything just said about human ingratitude.

The choice of shahid (شَهِيدٌ) rather than shahid creates rhetorical power. Shahid (fa’il pattern) suggests habitual action - one who constantly witnesses, not just once. At any moment, you can testify to this truth about yourself. The lam in “la-shahid” (لَشَهِيدٌ) again provides emphasis - truly, constantly a witness.

The grammatical structure makes humans both the subject (al-insan) and the witness (shahid) to their own crime. This creates what Western rhetoric calls antimetabole - a reversal where the accused becomes the witness against himself.

Ayah 8: The Causal Connection

The third “wa innahu” (وَإِنَّهُ) completes the trilogy. The lam in “li-hubbi” (لِحُبِّ) is lam al-ta’lil - the lam of causation. It explains WHY humans are kanud - “because of his love” or “due to his love.” This grammatical structure makes the love of wealth the driving cause of ingratitude.

The definiteness of “al-khair” (الْخَيْرِ) with the definite article suggests both “the good things” (material wealth) and “THE best” (superlative sense). The word choice is rhetorically broader than “mal” (money), encompassing any material pursuit of “the better.”

The lam in “la-shadid” (لَشَدِيدٌ) provides the third emphatic predicate in this section. The fa’il pattern (shadid) intensifies - not just “strong” but “extreme, severe, intense.” The rhetorical effect of this triple emphasis (ayahs 6, 7, 8 all with lam emphasis) hammers home the diagnosis of human nature.

Ayahs 9-10: The Interrogative and Passive Voice

The interrogative hamza in “a-fa-la ya’lamu” (أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ) creates a rhetorical question - not seeking information but rebuking. The fa- connector (“then”) links this logically to everything prior: “Given all this about human nature, is he not then aware…?”

The present/future tense “ya’lamu” (يَعْلَمُ) rather than past tense shifts the temporal focus. This isn’t about what was known, but about awareness of what’s coming.

The idha (إِذَا) particle indicates certain future time, not conditional “if” (law/in). This adds rhetorical force - not “if” graves are ransacked, but “when” (certainty) they are ransacked.

The passive voice in “bu’thira” (بُعْثِرَ) and “hussila” (حُصِّلَ) is rhetorically powerful. By omitting the agent (who ransacks? who retrieves?), Allah creates suspense and focuses attention on the action itself and its objects (graves, hearts). The passive construction also universalizes the statement - this will happen, regardless of who does it.

The Form II intensive in “hussila” (حُصِّلَ) rather than Form I “husila” emphasizes thoroughness - not just “retrieved” but “thoroughly extracted, completely obtained.” Nothing will be left behind or hidden.

Ayah 11: The Conclusive Declaration

The inna (إِنَّ) here provides conclusive authority - Allah’s own testimony after describing what will happen. After the passive voice kept the agent mysterious, Allah now reveals His own position.

The phrase “rabbahum bihim” (رَبَّهُم بِهِم) creates a grammatical and thematic link back to ayah 6’s “li-rabbihi” (لِرَبِّهِ). The same root r-b-b appears in both places, creating an envelope structure (inclusio): you were ungrateful to Rabb, but Rabb will have complete knowledge of you.

The temporal phrase “yawma’idhin” (يَوْمَئِذٍ) - “on that Day” - uses the compound form (yawma + idhin) to point specifically to the Day just described in ayahs 9-10. The idhin particle references back anaphorically.

The final predicate “la-khabir” (لَخَبِيرٌ) receives emphasis through the lam. The choice of khabir rather than ’alim is rhetorically precise - not just “knowing” but “experienced, thoroughly informed, aware of the source.” The fa’il pattern (khabir) intensifies the quality.

The juxtaposition is powerful: humans don’t know exactly where treasure is when they raid (ayahs 1-5), but Allah knows exactly what’s in every heart when His angels raid the graves (ayahs 9-11). The parallel scenes create a rhetorical bracket around the surah’s message.

Rhyme Scheme Shifts

The surah demonstrates sophisticated rhyme pattern shifts that mirror content changes. Ayahs 1-5 use the rhyme -an (dabhan, qadhan, subhan, naq’an, jam’an), creating a rhythmic, galloping sound that mimics horses in motion.

Ayahs 6-8 shift to the rhyme -id (kanud, shahid, shadid), creating a different sonic atmosphere - harder, more accusatory sounds for the indictment of human nature.

Ayahs 9-11 return to variations on -ur/-ir sounds (qubur, sudur, khabir), creating a third distinct sonic section for the eschatological conclusion.

These phonetic shifts mark the three-part structure of the surah, helping the listener track the transitions between battle scene, moral diagnosis and resurrection warning. The Arabs, who had memorized thousands of lines of poetry and were sensitive to sonic patterns, would immediately recognize these structural divisions through sound alone.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayahs 1-5: Desert Warfare Context

The imagery of these opening ayahs reflects the actual warfare practices of pre-Islamic Arabia. Raids (ghazw, pl. ghazawat) were a central feature of Bedouin life - tribal confederations would raid neighboring tribes to acquire wealth, camels, goods and prestige. These raids were formalized in tribal custom, with strict codes about when raids were permitted and what constituted honorable conduct.

The morning raid (al-mughirat subhan) described here reflects tactical reality. While night raids had the advantage of surprise, morning raids served different purposes. Dawn raids could be psychological warfare - showing the enemy you weren’t afraid to be seen, that you had the courage to attack in daylight. The morning timing also allowed raiders to navigate difficult terrain they’d crossed in darkness and to see the full scope of the enemy’s defenses.

Descending from hills (mughirat) was a common tactic. Raiders would position themselves overnight on high ground, waiting for dawn to see the enemy camp clearly, then charge downhill to gain gravitational speed advantage. The Quraysh were familiar with this - they themselves engaged in such raids and had defensive positions around Makkah to guard against being raided.

The detail of horses panting (dabhan) and causing sparks (muriyat qadhan) from their hooves striking rock is historically accurate to the Arabian terrain. The rocky desert landscape, particularly the volcanic rock fields (harrat) of the Hijaz region, would indeed produce sparks when struck by metal horseshoes at high speed.

Historically, Arabian horses were prized specifically for their loyalty and willingness to charge into danger. Unlike camels, which would flee from threat, war horses (khayl) were bred and trained for battle. Arabs composed extensive poetry praising their horses’ courage. The relationship between rider and mount was intense - a warrior’s life literally depended on his horse’s loyalty.

Ayah 6-8: Pre-Islamic Arab Values

The accusation that “man is kanud (ungrateful) to his Rabb” would have struck the original Makkan audience powerfully. The Quraysh were polytheists who acknowledged Allah as the supreme Creator (“If you ask them who created the heavens and earth, they will say ‘Allah’” - 29:61), but they were intensely materialistic. Their society was organized around trade, wealth accumulation and tribal honor defined by material prosperity.

Makkan society was stratified by wealth. The merchant aristocracy (like Abu Jahl, Abu Lahab, Walid ibn Mughira) controlled trade routes and accumulated vast wealth through the caravan trade between Yemen and Syria. This wealth gave them social and political power. Slavery was widespread, including debt slavery. The poor were marginalized.

The love of “khair” (wealth/the best) reflected historical reality. Arabs composed poetry boasting of their raids and plunder. Their concept of honor (sharaf) and nobility (hasab/nasab) was tied to generosity - but generosity displayed wealth, which came from trade or raiding. Pre-Islamic poetry (mu’allaqat) frequently celebrated material success.

The Quraysh’s devotion to their business interests was legendary. They had sacred months when warfare was prohibited (Muharram, Rajab, Dhul Qa’dah, Dhul Hijjah) specifically to protect trade caravans and allow safe passage for commerce. When Islam threatened their trade-based power structure, they resisted violently.

Historically, this surah came at a time when the Makkan elite were intensifying persecution of Muslims precisely because Islam challenged their economic system. Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم preached equality, care for the poor, and limitation of usury - all threatening to the merchant class’s wealth accumulation.

Ayahs 9-11: Jewish and Christian Eschatology

The imagery of graves being overturned (bu’thira ma fi al-qubur) has parallels in Jewish eschatological literature. The Hebrew Bible speaks of Sheol (the grave/underworld) giving up its dead: “Many who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake” (Daniel 12:2). The concept of bodily resurrection was debated in Second Temple Judaism - Pharisees affirmed it while Sadducees rejected it.

By the 7th century CE, bodily resurrection was established Christian doctrine. Christians in Arabia (including the Ghassanids in Syria and Christians in Najran) believed in the resurrection of the dead and final judgment. The Nicene Creed affirms: “We look for the resurrection of the dead and the life of the world to come.”

The Quranic emphasis that “what is in the chests will be retrieved” (hussila ma fi al-sudur) echoes biblical themes of God knowing the heart. In Jewish scripture: “The Lord searches every heart and understands every desire and every thought” (1 Chronicles 28:9). Christianity emphasizes: “Nothing in all creation is hidden from God’s sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of Him to whom we must give account” (Hebrews 4:13).

The phrase “their Rabb will be Khabir (All-Aware) of them” connects to the biblical divine attribute of omniscience. In Psalms: “You have searched me, Lord, and You know me… You perceive my thoughts from afar” (Psalm 139:1-2). The Quran’s khabir parallels biblical descriptions of God’s complete, intimate knowledge of human hearts.

However, there’s a Quranic innovation here: the connection between the raid imagery and resurrection creates a uniquely Quranic rhetorical move. The parallelism between worldly raiders searching for buried treasure and divine “raiders” (angels) searching graves for hearts is not found in biblical literature. This is distinctively Quranic wisdom - using the Arabs’ own cultural experience to illuminate spiritual reality.

Historical Context: Revelation in Makkah

Scholars agree this is an early Makkan surah based on its themes, structure and language. Early Makkan surahs characteristically: (1) use powerful oath structures, (2) focus on resurrection and judgment, (3) emphasize human accountability, (4) are brief and rhythmic, (5) address the Quraysh’s materialism and resistance to monotheism.

The surah’s emphasis on ingratitude and love of wealth directly addresses the Quraysh’s situation. They had become wealthy through trade but attributed their success to their own efforts and the intercession of idols at the Ka’bah, not to Allah’s provision. They resisted Muhammad’s صلى الله عليه وسلم message partly because accepting his prophethood would require redistributing wealth and power.

The surah’s stern warning about Judgment Day reflects the context of rejection. The Quraysh mocked the idea of resurrection: “When we are bones and dust, shall we really be raised up again?” (37:16). They demanded material proof, miracles, angels visibly descending. This surah responds by making resurrection visceral and certain - not “if” but “when” (idha) graves are overturned.

Historically, horses (khayl) were valuable assets in Arabia. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم later made statements about the merit of keeping horses for jihad. But in early Makkah, when Islam was purely a spiritual call with no military context yet, the image of loyal war horses served as a parable about spiritual loyalty to Allah - something the Quraysh lacked despite their wealth and power.

Coherence

Tripartite Structure: Three Unified Scenes

The surah demonstrates remarkable nazm (internal coherence) through its three-part structure, where each section is distinct yet intimately connected to the others. Section 1 (ayahs 1-5) presents the raid scene. Section 2 (ayahs 6-8) diagnoses human nature. Section 3 (ayahs 9-11) reveals the final raid. These aren’t random parts but a unified argument.

The coherence works through multiple layers. First, there’s the thematic progression: worldly action → moral reality → eschatological consequence. The first section shows what humans DO (raid for wealth). The middle section explains WHY they do it (ingratitude and love of material things). The final section reveals the RESULT (they’ll face a raid themselves).

Second, there’s character development: The human being is central throughout. In section 1, humans aren’t explicitly mentioned - we see only horses and their loyalty. In section 2, the focus shifts to “al-insan” (the human) and his disloyalty. In section 3, these same humans face the consequences - their graves raided, hearts exposed. The absent-present-judged pattern creates narrative coherence.

Third, there’s the brilliant double-meaning that unifies the whole. Every image in the first section works on two levels simultaneously: (1) a worldly raid the Arabs understood, and (2) the eschatological raid they needed to understand. This dual-layer isn’t coincidence - it’s the surah’s central coherence device.

The Horse-Human Parallel: Structural Coherence

The surah’s internal logic depends on the implicit comparison between horses and humans. Ayahs 1-5 describe horses’ unwavering loyalty - charging toward certain death for their master despite receiving minimal care (just food, shelter and hard work in return). These horses sacrifice even their survival instinct for the master who provided for them.

Ayah 6 suddenly declares: “Indeed, the human being is kanud (disloyal, ungrateful) to his Rabb.” The coherence is implicit but powerful - You just saw how loyal a horse is to its master who gave it so little. But you, human, are disloyal to YOUR Master who gave you EVERYTHING. The horse is the positive standard against which human failure is measured.

This comparison explains why the surah opens with horses rather than any other imagery. The choice isn’t arbitrary - it’s structurally necessary for the argument. Without the horse’s loyalty, the indictment of human disloyalty lacks its contrasting standard. Without seeing what a “good servant” looks like (the horse), we can’t fully grasp how much humans fall short.

The pronoun “their Rabb” (rabbahum) in ayah 11 echoes “his Rabb” (rabbihi) in ayah 6, creating grammatical coherence. The same relationship (created being to Rabb/Master) frames both the diagnosis (you’re disloyal to your Rabb) and the judgment (your Rabb will know everything about you).

The Two Raids: Structural Parallelism

The surah’s most sophisticated coherence device is the parallel structure between the opening raid (ayahs 1-5) and the closing raid (ayahs 9-11). Every element in the first scene has a corresponding element in the second:

First Raid (Worldly): - Raiders: few horses with human riders (al-’adiyat) - Target: enemy camp/town - Time: morning (subhan) - Action: descending from heights (mughirat) - Method: causing dust clouds (naq’an) for confusion - Goal: penetrate enemy ranks (wasatna bihi jam’an) - Purpose: search for buried treasure - Knowledge: raiders don’t know where treasure is - Uncertainty: might not find anything valuable

Final Raid (Eschatological): - Raiders: angels as ’adi (those who arrest for judgment) - Target: graves of the dead - Time: morning (implied - the sun rising from the west) - Action: overturning graves from above (bu’thira) - Method: causing dust clouds as graves are ransacked - Goal: retrieve what’s in the graves/chests - Purpose: extract the treasure of hearts (ma fi al-sudur) - Knowledge: Allah has khibrah - knows exactly where to look - Certainty: will definitely find what’s there and know its value

This structural parallelism creates profound coherence. The first scene isn’t just a random dramatic opening - it’s a prophetic preview of the second scene. The surah uses the familiar to illuminate the unfamiliar, the worldly to explain the spiritual, the visible to reveal the invisible.

The Chest/Heart Connection: Verbal Coherence

There’s a subtle but crucial verbal link between ayah 8 and ayah 10. Ayah 8 states: “wa innahu li-hubbi al-khairi la-shadid” - he is intense in his love (hubb) of wealth. Where does love (hubb) reside? In the qalb (heart), which is located in the sadr (chest).

Ayah 10 then says: “wa hussila ma fi al-sudur” - what is in the chests will be retrieved. The coherence is precise: Your love (hubb) of khair is in your chest (sadr), and that chest will be opened and its contents extracted (hussila). Will Allah find khair (goodness) in your chest, or will He find only hubb al-khair (obsessive love of material things)?

The word khair appears only in ayah 8 in this surah, making it verbally significant. It creates a test: you loved “khair” (wealth/the best) in this life; will there be any khair (goodness) found in your heart in the next life? The same word works on both levels - material and spiritual.

The word sudur (chests) in ayah 10 also evokes the “treasure chest” that raiders search for in the first scene. Worldly raiders ransack towns searching for treasure chests buried underground. Divine raiders ransack graves searching for the treasure chest (sadr) inside each body, which contains the ultimate treasure: the heart and its secrets.

The Witness Theme: Structural Coherence

Ayah 7 introduces the witness theme: “wa innahu ’ala dhalika la-shahid” - he is a witness to that (his own ingratitude). This self-testimony theme connects to the final ayah in a crucial way.

On Judgment Day, when “what is in the chests is retrieved” and “their Rabb is Khabir of them,” whose testimony will condemn you? Your own. The hearts’ contents that are extracted will testify against you. You are shahid (witness) now in this life if you’re honest with yourself about your ingratitude. Your heart will be shahid (evidence) against you in the next life.

The progression is coherent: You witness against yourself (7) → because your love of wealth is obvious in your actions (8) → and eventually your heart’s contents will be physically extracted as evidence (10) → and Allah will have complete experiential knowledge of that evidence (11).

Connection to Surah Placement (Number 100)

This is the 100th surah - located near the end of the mushaf but among the earliest revealed. Its placement near Surahs Al-Zilzal (99) and Al-Qari’ah (101) is coherent - all three deal with resurrection and judgment day imagery:

  • Al-Zilzal (99): Earth shaken, gives up its burdens, graves give up their dead
  • Al-’Adiyat (100): Graves ransacked, hearts extracted
  • Al-Qari’ah (101): The striking Hour, people like scattered moths

The sequence creates thematic coherence across surah boundaries. Al-Zilzal describes the earth’s earthquake and testimony. Al-’Adiyat describes the extraction from graves and hearts. Al-Qari’ah describes the scattering of people and weighing of deeds. Together, they form a comprehensive picture of resurrection’s stages.

Within the Quran’s broader structure, this surah’s themes connect to other passages about human ingratitude. “And man is most contentious” (18:54). “Indeed, mankind is ungrateful to his Lord” (100:6) echoes “And indeed, he is intense in his love of wealth” (89:20). These verbal and thematic echoes create trans-surah coherence.

The Triple Emphasis Pattern: Rhetorical Coherence

Ayahs 6-8 use a powerful triple emphasis pattern: - “inna al-insana li-rabbihi la-kanud” (6) - “wa innahu ’ala dhalika la-shahid” (7)
- “wa innahu li-hubbi al-khairi la-shadid” (8)

Each statement begins with (wa) innahu (“and indeed he”) and ends with an emphatic predicate using lam (la-kanud, la-shahid, la-shadid). This creates rhythmic coherence and rhetorical force - three hammer blows of accusation, each with the same grammatical structure, building to a climax.

The three-part indictment is logically coherent: 1. The fundamental problem: ingratitude to the Master 2. The self-evidence: he witnesses this truth about himself 3. The driving cause: intense love of material wealth

These aren’t three unrelated criticisms but three aspects of one coherent diagnosis. The cause (3) produces the effect (1), and the evidence (2) confirms both. The structural coherence strengthens the argument’s logical force.

Psychological, Political & Social

Human Psychology of Gratitude and Ingratitude

The surah presents a sophisticated psychological analysis of human nature through the concept of kanud. This isn’t simply “ingratitude” as a surface emotion - it’s a psychological pattern of disconnection from one’s source of provision. The human tendency toward kanud reveals several psychological dynamics:

First, there’s the psychology of familiarity breeding contempt. Allah provides constantly - breath, heartbeat, health, provision, family, opportunities. But because these come regularly, humans psychologically habituate to them. What arrives consistently loses its perceived value. The horse, by contrast, doesn’t habituate - it remains grateful for each feeding, each shelter, each day. This reflects what psychologists call “hedonic adaptation” - humans quickly adapt to positive circumstances and cease feeling grateful for them.

Second, there’s the psychology of attribution error. When good things happen, humans tend to attribute success to their own efforts (“I worked hard for this”) rather than to Allah’s enabling. When bad things happen, they blame Allah (“Why did God let this happen?”). The horse doesn’t fall into this error - it knows it’s entirely dependent on its master. This reflects what psychologists call “self-serving bias” - taking credit for successes while externalizing failures.

Third, there’s the psychology of selective attention. Ayah 8 reveals that humans are “shadid” (intense/extreme) in their love of khair (material gain/the best). This intensity of focus on what we want makes us psychologically blind to what we have. A person earning $50,000 obsesses over those making $100,000. The person earning $100,000 obsesses over millionaires. This is what psychologists call “upward social comparison” - we compare ourselves to those above us, never feeling satisfied.

The self-witness theme (ayah 7) reveals deep psychological insight. Every human knows their own ingratitude if they’re honest - “wa innahu ’ala dhalika la-shahid” (he is a witness to this about himself). This isn’t external accusation but internal testimony. Psychologically, this speaks to the phenomenon of conscience - humans possess inherent moral knowledge of their own failures. The only escape is to be kanud in another sense: to “cut off” this self-awareness, to suppress the conscience, to refuse self-reflection.

The Psychology of Materialism

The diagnosis “wa innahu li-hubbi al-khairi la-shadid” (he is intense in his love of wealth/material gain) captures the psychology of materialism with precision. The word shadid (intense/extreme/severe) suggests materialism isn’t a casual preference but an obsessive, all-consuming passion.

Psychologically, materialism functions as attempted self-medication for deeper spiritual emptiness. When humans are kanud (disconnected from their Rabb), they experience existential void. They attempt to fill this void with khair - possessions, status, “the best” of worldly things. But material things cannot fill a spiritual void, so the person becomes “shadid” - increasingly intense, never satisfied, always needing more.

The lecture highlighted modern examples: the person who works 70-80 hours per week, destroying health and relationships for wealth; the person who then needs to get drunk or high on weekends because they can’t live with themselves; the person who has no genuine human connections left because 99% of their time went to money.
